title: The Two Margins Every Queensland Café and Restaurant Owner Needs to Know Before July 1
linkedin:
A full booking sheet doesn't mean a profitable venue.
Most Queensland hospitality owners know this instinctively. Fewer have the numbers to back it up.

The pattern we see across cafés and restaurants at this point in the year: the EOFY stocktake gets completed for compliance, the BAS gets lodged, and that's where the exercise ends. But the same data, used properly, produces the actual food cost percentage for the year. Not the assumed one from when the menu was last priced. The real one after twelve months of supplier movements, portion drift, and ingredient cost changes.

For most venues, that number is higher than expected. Sometimes materially so.

A café at $900,000 in food revenue running at 36 percent cost instead of its target 32 percent carries $36,000 in recoverable annual margin. Not from new customers. From purchasing decisions, portion discipline, and prices that reflect current ingredient costs.

Then there's the July 1 labour reality. Award rates increase from the first full pay period of the new financial year. For a venue with casual employees on weekend shifts, the real payroll cost increase once loadings and penalty rates are applied is consistently higher than the headline percentage. Any pricing review needs to happen before July, because the new wage cost arrives on day one.

The venues that enter the new year with food cost understood from real EOFY data, labour percentage reviewed by shift type, and prices adjusted before the costs land are the ones whose margins hold through the year ahead.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#AustralianBusiness #BrisbaneSME #SMEFinance
facebook:
Quick question for any café or restaurant owners in Queensland: when did you last calculate your actual food cost percentage? Not the assumed one. The real number from opening stock, purchases, and closing stocktake.

For most venues, EOFY is when this gets done properly, if at all. And the number that comes back is usually higher than expected.

With award wages increasing from July 1, there's a real case for doing this review now and adjusting menu prices before the new cost structure arrives rather than after. Ten days to June 30, and the data is almost there.

#AustralianBusiness #Hospitality
google:
A note for café, restaurant, and bar owners across Brisbane and Queensland as the end of financial year approaches.

The EOFY stocktake is a compliance requirement. Your bookkeeper handles it, and it feeds accurately into your BAS and accounts. But the same stocktake data has a second use that most hospitality owners do not take up: it generates your real food cost percentage for the year.

Not the assumed percentage from when you last priced the menu. The actual figure after twelve months of supplier movements, portion changes, and new product substitutions.

For most venues, this number is higher than the owner believes it to be. The gap between the assumed food cost and the real one is, in most cases, a direct margin opportunity, recoverable through purchasing adjustments, portion discipline, and pricing that reflects current ingredient costs.

Layered on top of that, award wage rates under the Restaurant Industry Award increase from the first full pay period of July. For venues with casual employees on weekend and evening shifts, the real payroll cost movement once loadings are applied is consistently higher than the headline rate. A menu pricing review completed before 1 July recovers this cost from the start of the new financial year rather than chasing it from behind.

The new blog post on the ProfitPulse website walks through both margins, food cost percentage and labour percentage, and explains how to use the EOFY stocktake data to build the commercial picture before July arrives. Read the full piece on the blog.
